211.bio_narrative: Rosa Diaz is a 38-year-old Hispanic woman living in Houston, Texas, who works long shifts in a high-pressure public-facing role and prioritizes staying functional and independent. She has a history of essential hypertension and hyperlipidemia treated with atenolol and atorvastatin, and she reports strong adherence to her medication routine despite a demanding schedule. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol rarely. Her diet is generally balanced, although she notes stress-related overeating at times. Family history is notable for ischemic heart disease in a first-degree relative, which has increased her concern as symptoms progressed. Her current problem began on 01/10/2026 when she noticed intermittent substernal chest pressure during exertion (stairs/brisk walking). The discomfort is described as pressure/tightness rated 6–8/10, lasting 5–12 minutes, reliably triggered by activity and relieved by rest. Over the following weeks, episodes increased to several times per week and began occurring with lower levels of exertion, accompanied by shortness of breath and occasional diaphoresis, with intermittent radiation to the left arm. She denies pleuritic or positional chest pain and does not have reproducible chest wall tenderness. She sought evaluation with her primary provider, Dr. Erin Bush at Houston Methodist Hospital, and underwent a structured diagnostic workup. Serial resting ECGs (02/18/2026, 03/01/2026, and 03/20/2026) demonstrated persistent lateral ST-T abnormalities. An exercise treadmill stress test on 03/12/2026 reproduced her typical chest pressure and showed exercise-induced horizontal ST-segment depressions in lateral leads. A transthoracic echocardiogram on 03/15/2026 showed preserved left ventricular ejection fraction and no structural explanation for her symptoms. Laboratory testing on 03/14/2026 included lipid and metabolic risk profiling and a negative high-sensitivity troponin at the time of blood draw. Given persistent exertional symptoms with objective ischemic changes on stress ECG and corroborating serial ECG abnormalities, her cardiology consultant recommended myocardial perfusion imaging with SPECT (CPT 78452) to localize and quantify inducible ischemia and to support risk stratification and downstream management planning. The study is scheduled prospectively for 05/08/2026 at Houston Methodist Hospital Nuclear Cardiology.

210.bio_narrative: Jonah Simms is a 35-year-old male living in Houston, Texas, who has been followed for cardiovascular risk reduction in the setting of essential hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He has been consistent with clinic follow-ups and takes metoprolol (Lopressor) and atorvastatin (Lipitor) daily, along with low-dose aspirin for cardiovascular prevention. He works a predominantly desk-based job and has maintained regular exercise (walking and light resistance training) but has noticed that his exertional tolerance fluctuates when chest symptoms occur. Over the last year, Jonah has experienced intermittent substernal chest pressure and tightness that is most notable with exertion such as climbing stairs or brisk walking and sometimes during heightened anxiety. Episodes typically last several minutes and improve with rest, and he also reports dyspnea on exertion and intermittent palpitations during these events. Because symptoms persisted despite medication adherence and lifestyle modifications, he was evaluated by cardiology. A resting-only SPECT study was completed on 2026-02-20 and showed an inferior to inferolateral perfusion defect that raised concern for ischemia versus attenuation artifact; the study explicitly recommended stress imaging for diagnostic completion. A CMP obtained on 2026-03-01 demonstrated normal electrolytes, renal function, and liver enzymes, supporting readiness for monitored pharmacologic stress testing. Jonah’s family history is notable for ischemic heart disease in first-degree relatives, which increases his overall risk profile. Socially, he does not smoke, limits alcohol to social settings, and is motivated to follow dietary guidance, including reduced saturated fats and mindful sodium intake. He receives monthly medication management for generalized anxiety disorder, which he reports helps with symptom coping, but his exertional chest symptoms continue and require objective cardiac evaluation. Given ongoing chest pain and shortness of breath with exertion, cardiovascular risk factors, and a prior resting perfusion abnormality that cannot be fully interpreted without a stress component, cardiology has requested a stress/rest myocardial perfusion SPECT study (CPT 78452). The goal is to quantify inducible ischemia and support informed decisions regarding ongoing medical therapy and any additional cardiac diagnostic evaluation.

213.bio_narrative: Chandler Bing is a 37-year-old male living in Dallas, Texas with a history of essential hypertension (I10) and hyperlipidemia (E78.5). Over the past several months he has developed intermittent substernal chest tightness provoked by exertion (R07.89), especially during jogging, brisk walking, or climbing multiple flights of stairs. The episodes typically last a few minutes, are relieved by rest, and are accompanied by mild shortness of breath when they occur. He has been consistent with prescribed metoprolol and atorvastatin and has continued weekly cardiac rehabilitation, but he reports that he sometimes limits exercise intensity to avoid triggering symptoms. His initial evaluation on 12/10/2025 documented exertional chest discomfort occurring during workouts and prompted a structured diagnostic workup. He completed an exercise treadmill stress test on 02/02/2026 (CPT 93015), during which his typical chest tightness was reproduced and ECG changes raised concern for ischemia; this is captured as an abnormal cardiovascular function study (R94.39). A follow-up resting ECG on 02/08/2026 (CPT 93000) demonstrated nonspecific ST-T abnormalities. In a cardiology specialist consultation on 02/15/2026, given persistent exertional symptoms and functional study findings, his cardiologist recommended coronary anatomic assessment with Cardiac CT Angiography (CTA) (CPT 75574) to evaluate for obstructive versus non-obstructive coronary artery disease and to guide subsequent management decisions. Chandler works in an office-based role and maintains a structured routine that includes planned exercise and attention to diet. He does not smoke, has no history of illicit drug use, and limits alcohol to social occasions. Family history is notable for ischemic heart disease in a first-degree relative (Z82.49), which adds to his risk profile. In preparation for contrast-enhanced CTA, recent laboratory testing confirmed stable renal function, and pre-procedure planning has focused on heart-rate optimization for image quality and safe completion of the study. The CTA is scheduled to provide detailed assessment of coronary anatomy and plaque burden in the setting of ongoing exertional chest pain and abnormal prior testing.

265.bio_narrative: Lisa Cuddy is a 57-year-old woman living in Austin, Texas, who works in healthcare administration and is accustomed to managing stress and long workdays. Over the last 7–8 months she developed increasingly frequent heartburn and burning epigastric pain (K21.9, R10.13), initially responsive to over-the-counter antacids but gradually becoming daily and worse at night. She has been consistent with lifestyle measures such as avoiding late meals and elevating the head of her bed. Despite an adequate therapeutic trial of pantoprazole 40 mg twice daily and adjunct sucralfate, she continues to have significant symptoms. Approximately 4 months before the planned procedure date, she noticed progressive difficulty swallowing solid foods (R13.10). She describes a sensation of food sticking in the mid-chest several times per week, requiring water to clear, and she has begun limiting portions to avoid discomfort. This change in intake has been accompanied by unintentional weight loss of roughly 12–14 pounds (R63.4). In the 6–8 weeks prior to gastroenterology consultation, she developed marked fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance and reported intermittent black, tarry stools (K92.1). Laboratory testing on 2026-03-10 demonstrated iron deficiency anemia (D50.9) with low hemoglobin and ferritin, supporting chronic blood loss as a concern. A CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast did not identify an acute intra-abdominal process but could not evaluate mucosal disease. Her social history includes occasional alcohol use and no current tobacco or illicit drug use. She previously used NSAIDs intermittently for aches and headaches and stopped once anemia and melena were recognized. Family history is notable for a father with esophageal reflux complications and a maternal aunt with colorectal cancer; these factors, combined with her alarm symptoms, increased concern for significant upper GI pathology. Given refractory reflux/epigastric pain and the combination of dysphagia, weight loss, and objective iron deficiency anemia with suspected upper GI bleeding, gastroenterology recommended an Upper GI Endoscopy with Biopsy (EGD) (CPT 43239) to directly visualize the esophagus, stomach, and duodenum and obtain tissue samples to define the underlying cause and guide next steps in care.

272.bio_narrative: Olenna Sand is a 70-year-old widowed woman living independently in Dallas, Texas. She is known by her family as decisive and private, preferring to handle problems quietly before asking for help. She previously worked as an office manager for a small wholesale business and remains active in her neighborhood association, though her energy has declined over the past several months. She drives herself to appointments and relies on her niece (emergency contact) for occasional errands and transportation support when needed. Over the last 4–6 months, she developed a persistent change in bowel habits (R19.4) with alternating constipation and looser stools, increased urgency, and intermittent crampy generalized abdominal pain (R10.84), often worse after meals. Beginning around January 2026, she noticed intermittent bright red blood per rectum (K62.5), sometimes mixed with stool and sometimes present only on the toilet paper. She also developed progressive fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance, limiting her usual walking routine. She denied heavy NSAID use and reported no prior colonoscopy records available in her current chart. Her primary care evaluation in February 2026 initiated diagnostic testing. Fecal immunochemical testing returned heme-positive (R19.5), and laboratory results demonstrated microcytic anemia with iron studies confirming iron deficiency (D50.9), raising concern for chronic gastrointestinal blood loss. A CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast was obtained to evaluate persistent abdominal pain and to exclude acute pathology; it showed mild sigmoid diverticulosis but no acute inflammatory process, acknowledging that CT can miss mucosal lesions. She started oral iron and conservative bowel management (hydration and fiber trial), but objective findings and symptoms persisted. Family history is notable for a father with colorectal cancer diagnosed in his early 70s and a mother with long-standing anemia of unclear etiology, increasing the importance of definitive evaluation. Given the combination of rectal bleeding, heme-positive stool, iron deficiency anemia, and sustained bowel habit change, Gastroenterology recommended a diagnostic colonoscopy with biopsy (CPT 45380) to evaluate the colonic mucosa and obtain tissue diagnosis when indicated to guide next-step care planning. Pre-procedure evaluation addressed sedation readiness and medication holds, and she began physical therapy for deconditioning related to fatigue while the diagnostic workup proceeds.

273.bio_narrative: Severus Black is a 69-year-old divorced man living alone in Houston, Texas, who presents as a new gastroenterology patient for evaluation prior to a requested colonoscopy with biopsy (CPT 45380). Over the past 6–8 months he has experienced a gradual change in bowel habits (R19.4), describing fewer spontaneous bowel movements with intermittent episodes of loose stools after several days of constipation (K59.00). He reports a persistent sensation of incomplete evacuation and has noted intermittent bright red blood on toilet paper and streaking on stool (K62.5), initially sporadic but occurring about 1–2 times weekly by early 2026. He also describes increasing fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance during routine walks. He works part-time as a museum archives assistant, preferring structured routines and quiet environments. He typically prepares meals at home and has recently reduced red meat intake while trying to increase fiber and fluids. He denies illicit drug use and drinks alcohol occasionally. He is a former smoker, quitting over a decade ago. His support system includes a close family member who assists with appointments and transportation when needed. Family history is notable for colorectal cancer in a maternal uncle diagnosed in his early 60s and colon polyps in his father in later life, which heightened the patient’s concern when bleeding persisted. He has not undergone prior colonoscopy or stool-based colorectal screening. In March 2026, he established care with primary care for rectal bleeding and bowel habit changes. Conservative treatment was initiated, including polyethylene glycol and dietary fiber strategies, along with topical therapy for suspected hemorrhoidal irritation. Despite these measures, symptoms continued. Laboratory testing showed an iron deficiency anemia pattern (D50.9) with low hemoglobin and low ferritin/iron saturation, raising concern for chronic gastrointestinal blood loss. He was started on oral iron supplementation. Given the persistent hematochezia, change in bowel habits, and objective anemia pattern without a prior colonoscopy, gastroenterology consultation recommended diagnostic colonoscopy with biopsy to evaluate for mucosal disease and structural lesions, and to obtain histology as needed to guide further management and follow-up planning.

274.bio_narrative: Monica Geller is a 60-year-old woman living in Dallas, Texas, who has generally been organized about her health maintenance but tends to push through symptoms while juggling household responsibilities and part-time catering work. Over the last 6–8 weeks she developed progressive fatigue and decreased exercise tolerance, along with intermittent epigastric burning that is worse after coffee and late meals. She also noticed her stools were occasionally darker, though she has not had hematemesis or overt bright red blood. Because the fatigue began to interfere with her ability to work long shifts on her feet, she presented to primary care for evaluation. At her 2026-02-24 office visit, evaluation focused on possible occult gastrointestinal blood loss. A fecal occult blood test returned positive (R19.5), and labs on 2026-03-10 confirmed microcytic iron deficiency anemia (D50.9) with low ferritin, low serum iron, and low transferrin saturation. She additionally reports early satiety and episodic reflux symptoms consistent with GERD (K21.9) and epigastric pain (R10.13). In the months preceding symptom onset, she used over-the-counter ibuprofen most days for knee pain, making NSAID-related mucosal injury a concern (Z79.1). She began a proton pump inhibitor (pantoprazole) and reduced NSAID use, with partial reduction in burning but persistent fatigue and continued concern for occult bleeding. Socially, she lives with her spouse and has reliable transportation and support for outpatient procedures. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol only occasionally. Family history includes a father with peptic ulcer disease in his 50s and a mother with iron deficiency anemia attributed to chronic gastritis; this background increased her concern when she saw her lab results. During a gastroenterology consultation on 2026-03-14, the combination of positive FOBT and iron deficiency anemia was reviewed in detail, and an outpatient diagnostic esophagogastroduodenoscopy was recommended to evaluate for an upper GI source of bleeding such as erosive gastritis, peptic ulcer disease, or other mucosal lesions. The endoscopic plan includes careful documentation of any visible mucosal abnormality and collection of tissue samples when endoscopic appearance indicates biopsy is appropriate to clarify etiology and guide management planning.

images.findings: Two-view chest radiograph obtained as part of pre-procedure assessment prior to the requested diagnostic EGD with sedation. Cardiomediastinal silhouette is within normal limits. No focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. Mild thoracic spine degenerative changes noted. Impression: No acute cardiopulmonary abnormality to limit planned outpatient endoscopy.

reports.results: Stool guaiac testing positive on 3 of 3 cards. Patient reports intermittent darker stools but no overt hematemesis. Result supports evaluation for occult gastrointestinal blood loss in preparation for requested diagnostic EGD (CPT 43235).
reports.notes: Patient notified same day; instructed to stop NSAIDs, start/continue acid suppression, and proceed with lab evaluation.

reports.results: Hgb 10.2 g/dL (low), Hct 32.1% (low), MCV 76 fL (low), RDW 16.9% (high), WBC 6.4 K/µL, platelets 412 K/µL (mildly high). Pattern consistent with microcytic anemia; supports need to evaluate for occult GI bleeding source prior to requested diagnostic EGD (CPT 43235).
reports.notes: No leukocytosis. Mild thrombocytosis can be reactive in iron deficiency.

reports.results: Ferritin 9 ng/mL (low), serum iron 28 µg/dL (low), TIBC 452 µg/dL (high), transferrin saturation 6% (low). Findings confirm iron deficiency anemia and support GI evaluation for bleeding source with requested diagnostic EGD (CPT 43235).
reports.notes: Oral iron started 2026-03-11; constipation prophylaxis recommended.

reports.results: Hgb 10.4 g/dL, Hct 32.8%, MCV 77 fL, platelets 398 K/µL. Hemoglobin stable compared with 2026-03-10; anemia persists, supporting continued evaluation for occult blood loss with scheduled diagnostic EGD (CPT 43235).
reports.notes: Results sent to GI office and anesthesia coordinator for procedural planning.
